title: SEGMENT 1 — Opening: the most abundant biological entities on Earth

lines.text: SEGMENT 1 — Opening: the most abundant biological entities on Earth
Alright, let's get started.
Good morning, everyone.
Today and for the next two hours, we're going to talk about bacteriophages — viruses that infect bacteria — and we're going to talk about them in a slightly unusual way.
We're not going to spend the whole two hours on basic phage biology, although we will get to that.

We're going to spend most of the time on phages as tools — as the substrate on which the entire field of molecular biology was built, as therapeutic agents making an unlikely comeback against antibiotic-resistant infections, as delivery vehicles, as research instruments, as the ancestral source material for CRISPR.
The phage is the most useful virus you have probably never had a personal encounter with — except, of course, that you've had trillions of them inside you continuously since you were a few days old.

If you walk out of here in two hours and you can do four things — first, tell me what a bacteriophage is and explain in plain English the difference between lytic and lysogenic lifestyles; second, sketch how the original molecular biology was done on T-even phages and lambda, and why phage was the right substrate for that work; third, articulate why phage therapy was abandoned in the West, why it survived in the East, and why it's coming back now; and fourth, list at least three modern biotech
applications of phages beyond therapy — display, food safety, delivery, CRISPR origins, vaccine vehicles, take your pick — then we have succeeded.
Everything else, the historical color, the molecular detail, the regulatory weeds, hangs off those four.
So let me start with the single most arresting fact in this entire field, because once you internalize it, you'll never think about microbiology quite the same way.
Bacteriophages are the most abundant biological entities on Earth.
Not the most abundant viruses — the most abundant entities, period.
The current best estimate for the global phage count is about ten to the thirty-first — that's a one followed by thirty-one zeros.
To put that in some kind of perspective, the number of stars in the observable universe is somewhere between ten to the twenty-second and ten to the twenty-fourth.
So there are, conservatively, more phages on Earth than there are stars in the visible universe — by something like seven orders of magnitude.
Pause.
More phages than stars.
Let that sit for a second.
Where are they all?
They are everywhere there are bacteria, and there are bacteria essentially everywhere.
There are phages in the ocean — a milliliter of seawater contains roughly ten million phage particles, and the global ocean is, by particle count, more virus than fish.
There are phages in soil.
There are phages in extreme environments — hot springs, salt flats, the deep subsurface.
And there are phages in you.

Your gut contains somewhere in the neighborhood of ten to the twelfth phage particles — a trillion phages, give or take, in your intestines right now, locked in a continuous, ancient predator-prey dance with your gut bacteria.
Your skin has them.
Your mouth has them.
Your lungs have them.
There is no phage-free part of you, unless you count the inside of healthy organs, and even there it's debatable.
Here's the analogy I want you to hold for the next two hours.
Phages are not broad-spectrum poisons.
They are precision predators.
When we talk later about phage therapy, I want you to feel the contrast with antibiotics.

An antibiotic is a chemical that, once you swallow it, distributes through your body and kills any bacterium of the sort it can kill — your pathogen, yes, but also your harmless commensals, your gut symbionts, the bacteria on your skin and in your mouth that you depend on.
It's a chemical carpet bomb, calibrated for selective toxicity against bacteria as a category but not for selectivity between species.
A phage, by contrast, is a hunter.
It infects one species — sometimes only one strain within a species.
It replicates only where its prey is.
It dies out when its prey is gone.
It does not touch your commensals.
It does not linger in tissues.
It is, in a real and biological sense, the opposite kind of antimicrobial — precise where antibiotics are sweeping, targeted where antibiotics are broad.
That contrast — broad-spectrum chemical poisons versus precision biological predators — is going to be the central frame for the therapy half of this lecture.
And the contrast between phages as the original molecular biology subject and phages as a forgotten tool that's now coming back is going to be the central frame for the history.
Keep both of those frames in your head and we'll be fine.
One more orienting fact before we get into the biology.
Phages are old.
Older than us, older than mammals, older than the trees.
They have been infecting bacteria for somewhere between three and four billion years.
Every piece of bacterial biochemistry you can name has been shaped by the selective pressure of phages.
Bacterial restriction enzymes — the molecular scissors that became the foundation of recombinant DNA — exist because bacteria evolved them to chop up incoming phage DNA.
CRISPR-Cas — the genome editing system you've heard about in the CRISPR lecture — is a bacterial immune system against phages.
The reason these biotech tools exist at all is because bacteria and phages have been at war since deep time, and we're picking up the weapons left on the battlefield.
We've done this before, by the way.
The antibiotic story — soil microbes evolving chemical weapons over millions of years, us showing up and harvesting them — is the same shape of story.
The biotech revolution is, to a remarkable degree, the consequence of evolutionarily ancient microbial conflicts.
Alright.
With that in mind, let's build a phage.
